items.title: A pet insurer that grew revenue 25x since 2012 — and insiders just loaded up
items.idea: Compounding Quality's Pieter toured Seattle meeting the CEOs of Judges Scientific, Kelly Partners Group, Chapters Group, and Markel, then visited a US pet insurer whose revenue is up 25x since 2012, where insiders bought heavily this year and still hold 5% of the company. The stock is reportedly up 30% over the past month, and the firm is weighing a formal stake for its community.
items.angle: No ticker was named, but insider ownership at 5% plus a 25x revenue run since 2012 plus a 'we might buy this' signal from a value-investing shop is exactly the kind of lead worth chasing down before it's public.
items.tweetA: A value investor just told me his fund toured a US pet insurer whose revenue is up 25x since 2012, insiders still own 5% and bought more this year, and the stock is up 30% in a month. He won't say the name yet. That's the tell.
items.tweetB: US pet insurance penetration is still under 5% vs 25%+ in the UK and Sweden. A 25x revenue run since 2012 with insiders buying is what that S-curve looks like from the inside.

charts.desc: Photo of CEO meetings, no data chart

items.title: Warsh at Sintra signals the Fed is about to shrink itself
items.idea: Ironsides flags that incoming Fed Chair Warsh's Sintra comments point to a classic-liberal turn: less forward guidance, a smaller balance sheet footprint, an end to Reserve Management Purchases, and potentially outright MBS sales. They frame this against diverging global central bank starting points (Fed, ECB, BOE, BOC, BOJ all facing different inflation/fiscal conditions), citing Japan as the cautionary tale of a central bank's profitability blind spot after decades of easy money.
items.angle: A Fed ending RMPs and floating outright MBS sales is tightening the plumbing even while cutting the policy rate — duration and term-premium risk doesn't disappear just because the Fed cuts in September.
items.tweetA: The next Fed chair's playbook, per his own Sintra remarks: shrink the balance sheet, sell MBS outright, stop forward guidance. That's tightening liquidity while cutting rates. The curve won't like both signals at once.
items.tweetB: Everyone's debating Fed cuts. Nobody's pricing that the same Fed is about to end Reserve Management Purchases and start selling MBS. Duration is being tightened from the supply side, not just the rate side.

charts.desc: No chart in email — article likely has yield curve / balance sheet visuals, worth opening

items.title: Healthcare is adding jobs. Tech and finance are cutting them — at higher wages.
items.idea: June household employment contracted sharply and labor force participation fell — unemployment declined for the wrong reason, as workers left the labor force rather than found jobs. Ironsides notes wage growth is concentrated in low-wage healthcare hiring, while tech and finance show employment contraction alongside stronger wages, a sign productivity and AI adoption are reshaping labor demand.
items.angle: This is the labor-market fingerprint of AI adoption: shrinking headcount plus rising wages in white-collar sectors reads as a margin story, not the simple 'soft labor market equals dovish Fed' read most people default to.
items.tweetA: Tech and finance headcount is shrinking while their wages rise. That's not a slowing economy, that's AI letting companies cut headcount and pay the survivors more. Margin story, not a growth scare.
items.tweetB: Unemployment fell in June for the worst possible reason: people left the labor force, they didn't find jobs. Healthcare is absorbing them at low wages. That's not the labor market recovery the cut-in-September crowd thinks it is.

items.title: Ironsides: AI capex rate-of-change is peaking, leadership about to rotate to industrials
items.idea: Earnings and capex remain strong, but Ironsides sees the rate of change peaking, especially in AI infrastructure and data centers. They expect leadership to rotate from AI producers toward broader industrial and manufacturing beneficiaries, citing survey data, private credit pressure, and underperformance among major infrastructure spenders as early saturation signals.
items.angle: Pairs directly with the Dark Side of the Boom token-spend data below — two independent sources converging on the same rotation from AI producers toward whoever monetizes the infrastructure differently.
items.tweetA: Two macro shops in one week are independently flagging the same rotation: away from AI capex producers, toward the industrials and manufacturers who actually use the infrastructure. Different data, same conclusion.
items.tweetB: Private credit pressure plus underperformance in the biggest AI infra spenders is the market quietly pricing 'rate of change peaked,' not 'AI is over.' Second derivative matters more than the headline capex number.

items.title: Renaissance turned away tens of billions because Sharpe above 4 is a warning sign
items.idea: Medallion capped near $10bn, generating roughly 66% gross / 39% net returns from 1988-2018, refusing outside capital since 2005 — while RenTec's open, investable funds merely track the S&P. The piece formalizes why: cost scales with the square root of order size relative to daily volume, so doubling an order multiplies cost by about 1.4x, and a Sharpe above 4 almost always signals a strategy that cannot scale. Medallion at $10bn generating about $6.6bn/year of gross profit is a far better business than a $60bn fund earning 8%.
items.angle: Best explanation I've seen for why 'the best fund you can't invest in' and 'the fund that will take your money' are never the same fund — capacity, not talent, is the moat, and headline AUM is close to worthless as a quality signal.
items.tweetA: $6.6B of gross profit on a $10B fund vs 8% on a $60B fund. Same industry, wildly different businesses. AUM is a vanity metric — capacity discipline is the actual moat.
items.tweetB: Rule of thumb hiding in the math: any strategy with Sharpe above 4 is basically confessing it can't hold size. If a fund's edge looks too good, ask what happens when they 10x the book — that's the real question, not the backtest.

charts.desc: No chart embedded — article likely has a capacity/impact curve, worth opening

items.title: Every Chinese imperial dynasty lasted 250-290 years. Noah Smith: America just hit that number too.
items.idea: Noahpinion notes the Qing lasted 268 years, the Ming 276, the Tang 289 — and the US, marking 250 years today, shows the same symptoms of sclerosis that preceded each dynasty's terminal phase: local veto power blocking or multiplying the cost of factories, housing, energy and infrastructure, and immigration or even city-to-city migration turning into zero-sum fights over a fixed housing supply.
items.angle: If NIMBY-driven sclerosis is the modern equivalent of imperial court paralysis, the investable read is that the whole 'reindustrialization of America' thesis depends on breaking exactly the veto points this piece describes — permitting reform, not policy announcements, is the tell to watch.
items.tweetA: Qing: 268 years. Ming: 276. Tang: 289. The US just turned 250 and by Noah Smith's account is showing the same disease those dynasties had before the end: local vetoes blocking anything from getting physically built. Permitting reform is the actual reshoring trade.
items.tweetB: Every reshoring thesis assumes America can still build. The dynastic parallel here is that empires don't die from external enemies, they die from internal veto points multiplying build costs for decades. Watch state-level permitting law, not tariff headlines.

charts.desc: Embedded GDP trend chart in the post — worth opening

items.title: Fertility is below replacement everywhere, right as cognition shifts from brains to data centers
items.idea: Human population is set to dwindle as fertility falls below replacement in every region globally, while an increasing share of cognitive work shifts from human brains to data centers. Noahpinion argues nation-states may lose the coordinating role they've played for 250 years once that transition completes — calling this the end of the human age, not just the American one.
items.angle: This reframes 'peak human' as a demographic story and an AI-compute story happening simultaneously — a shrinking, aging global workforce plus compute as the substitute is a structural tailwind for automation and robotics that doesn't depend on any single AI stock's valuation or on AGI hype.
items.tweetA: Fertility is below replacement in every region on earth, for the first time ever, at the exact moment more cognition is shifting from brains to data centers than at any point in history. That's not two separate stories, it's one substitution trade.
items.tweetB: If population is shrinking everywhere and compute is the substitute, the labor-replacing-robotics thesis doesn't need AGI hype to work. It just needs demographics to keep doing what they're already doing.

charts.desc: Embedded fertility/GDP charts in the post — worth opening

items.title: The US has 40% of the world's top-cited scientists. No other country cracks 10%.
items.idea: Maverick's July 4 note cites: the US has led the world in science Nobel Prizes from 1905-2025; nearly 40% of the world's top 200,000 most-cited scientists are US-based versus no other country above 10%; half of science Nobel Prizes over the last decade went to US-based researchers; and 27% of US-based science Nobel winners from 1940-2020 were born outside the US.
items.angle: This is the immigration/talent-magnet argument for US exceptionalism stated as hard data rather than sentiment — worth pairing against the Noahpinion sclerosis thesis above: the 'secret sauce' of importing top talent is exactly the mechanism that housing and permitting sclerosis threatens to choke off.
items.tweetA: 40% of the world's most-cited scientists are in the US. No other country is above 10%. 27% of US science Nobel winners since 1940 were born somewhere else. The 'secret sauce' has a name: immigration policy.
items.tweetB: Read this next to the sclerosis argument going around today: America's edge is importing the world's top talent, and the same permitting and visa dysfunction that blocks factories also throttles the talent pipeline. Same disease, one more symptom.

charts.desc: Nobel Prize winners by country, 1905-2025 — US far ahead of every other nation

items.title: Russia just made its CBDC mandatory for systemically important banks, starting September 1
items.idea: Per the crypto roundup, the Bank of Russia says the digital ruble is ready for nationwide rollout, and systemically important banks and large retailers must support it starting September 1.
items.angle: One of the most aggressive CBDC mandates issued anywhere — a hard compliance date, not a pilot — and a live de-dollarization data point sitting right next to the Korean firms disputing their dollar-stablecoin alliance membership below. Opposite ends of the same currency-sovereignty fight.
items.tweetA: Russia isn't piloting a CBDC anymore. September 1, systemically important banks and large retailers are legally mandated to support the digital ruble. That's a compliance deadline, not a research paper.
items.tweetB: Same week: Russia mandates its CBDC by law, and Korean firms are publicly disowning being added to a dollar-stablecoin alliance without consent. Two data points on the same fight over who controls settlement rails.

items.title: CLARITY Act final Senate text expected today, floor vote in 2-4 weeks
items.idea: Per the roundup, the CLARITY Act's final Senate text is expected July 4, with a floor vote projected for July 20-30 — the market-structure bill that would formally split SEC/CFTC jurisdiction over crypto.
items.angle: A firm floor-vote window (July 20-30) is a real tradeable calendar catalyst for the entire crypto market-structure complex, not a vague 'regulation coming' headline to file away.
items.tweetA: CLARITY Act floor vote window: July 20-30. That's a three-week catalyst calendar for the entire crypto market-structure trade, not a vague 'regulation coming' headline.
items.tweetB: Market structure clarity has been the single biggest crypto policy overhang since 2022. There's now an actual date range. Position before the news, not after it.

items.title: Samsung and Dunamu say they were added to a stablecoin alliance without being asked
items.idea: Samsung Electronics, Dunamu and other Korean firms say they were listed as Open USD alliance members without formal consultation.
items.angle: Getting publicly named into a dollar-stablecoin alliance without consent is a governance red flag for whoever runs Open USD, and a reminder that stablecoin 'alliance' press releases are marketing artifacts until the named members confirm them.
items.tweetA: Samsung and Dunamu just said in public: we never agreed to be in your stablecoin alliance. That's not a rounding error, that's a governance problem for whoever's building Open USD.
items.tweetB: Every stablecoin alliance announcement should now get the same question: did the named members actually sign anything? Samsung and Dunamu just answered no, in public.

items.title: Only Meta discloses both direct and indirect water use from its AI data centers
items.idea: Per the AI News roundup, AI data centers may use far more water than tech giants disclose — only Meta reports both direct and indirect water use, while Microsoft, Google and Amazon mainly disclose direct use only.
items.angle: If indirect water use (power generation, upstream cooling supply chains) is systematically excluded from three of the four biggest disclosures, the real water footprint of the AI buildout is understated across the board — an ESG and regulatory risk that hasn't been priced anywhere.
items.tweetA: Microsoft, Google and Amazon report direct water use from AI data centers. Only Meta reports indirect too. That means 3 of the 4 biggest AI buildouts are only telling you part of the number.
items.tweetB: Water is becoming the second bottleneck of the AI trade after power. And the disclosure standard right now is pick whichever number makes you look better. That's a subpoena waiting to happen.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: The AI 'toll booth' index just fell 20% from its May peak
items.idea: The LLM Token Expenditure Index, tracking what users are effectively paying for AI usage, has fallen almost 20% from its May peak after nearly doubling since December. The author frames token spend as the toll booth on the AI highway: chipmakers sell shovels, hyperscalers build the road, but token spend is the only signal of whether customers still pay to use it. The read is either a healthy mix-shift toward cheaper inference, or the first sign that pricing power, not chip supply, is where the AI trade breaks.
items.angle: Lines up directly with Ironsides' capex-rotation call above — a falling toll-booth index plus AI capex rate-of-change peaking is two independent sources converging on the same thesis: the market repricing training-glamour toward inference-workhorse economics.
items.tweetA: Token spend is the only real revenue signal in the AI trade, and it just fell 20% from May's peak after nearly doubling since December. Valuations don't need an AI bust to wobble. They just need this line to stay down.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching GPU shipments as the AI health check. The actual toll booth is what customers pay per token, and it's rolling over. Training is the racehorse, inference is the workhorse — the market may be about to reprice which one it's paying for.

charts.desc: No chart in email — article almost certainly has the Token Expenditure Index chart itself, worth opening

items.title: Seven c: the trenches are risk on again, but the actual smart-money data got cut off in my preview
items.idea: Seven c's newsletter, which tracks on-chain smart money via ChainEDGE, DeFiLlama and TokenUnlocks, opens this week's issue by declaring the low-cap trenches risk on again, tracking the top 0.01% profitable DEX traders' weekly inflows and outflows — but the specific holdings list wasn't included in what I could read.
items.angle: Worth opening directly for the actual smart-money holdings table; the framing alone (risk on again in the degen trenches after a quiet stretch) is a sentiment data point worth cross-checking against on-chain flow data elsewhere.
items.tweetA: One on-chain tracker just called the crypto trenches risk-on again. If degen low-cap flow is turning on, that's usually a leading sentiment indicator for the rest of the market by one to two weeks.
items.tweetB: Smart-money DEX flow trackers are a noisy signal individually, but risk on in the trenches after a quiet stretch is exactly the kind of turn worth watching for confirmation elsewhere this week.

charts.desc: Data table inside — worth opening (preview cut off before the actual holdings list)

items.title: Japan's 'rental family' business is real — the movie about it says more about the loneliness economy than any earnings call
items.idea: The newsletter reviews Rental Family (starring Brendan Fraser), about Japan's real stand-in-family-member agencies, where people rent a father, husband or friend for events or emotional support. The reviewer highlights the emotional core: clients look at you like they've been waiting for you their whole lives.
items.angle: Japan's rental-family industry is a real, monetizing response to demographic isolation — shrinking families, an aging population, urban loneliness — the same demographic collapse Noahpinion flags globally today already spawning literal rent-a-relative businesses in the country furthest along that curve.
items.tweetA: Japan has a real industry renting out fake family members. A movie just put a human face on it. If you want to see what demographic collapse looks like as a P&L line item in 15 years, this is the preview.
items.tweetB: Loneliness-as-a-service already exists and has a real market in Japan. Every other aging economy — Korea, China, eventually the US — is on the same demographic path. That's not a niche movie plot, that's a market map.
